title: Maximum & minimum prices
summary:
## Why governments control prices

Governments intervene in markets to change the price/quantity outcome for reasons of **equity, affordability or protection**. The two main direct controls are **maximum prices** and **minimum prices**.

## Maximum (ceiling) prices

A **maximum price** is a legal *upper limit* on the price of a good, set to make essentials affordable (e.g. rent controls, capped food/fuel prices).

- To have any effect it must be set **below the free-market equilibrium**.
- Result: quantity demanded > quantity supplied → a **shortage (excess demand)**.
- **Consequences:** queues, waiting lists, rationing, black (parallel) markets where the good is resold above the cap, and a fall in quality.

> Diagram: price line below equilibrium; the horizontal gap between the S and D curves at that price is the shortage.

**To ease the shortage** the government can shift supply right (subsidise producers, release buffer stocks) or ration fairly.

## Minimum (floor) prices

A **minimum price** is a legal *lower limit*, set to protect producers' incomes or discourage consumption (e.g. agricultural price supports, a national minimum wage, minimum alcohol pricing).

- To have any effect it must be set **above the free-market equilibrium**.
- Result: quantity supplied > quantity demanded → a **surplus (excess supply)**.
- **Consequences:** unsold surpluses, government cost of buying up the surplus, wasted resources, and possible over-production.

> Diagram: price line above equilibrium; the horizontal gap between S and D at that price is the surplus.

## Buffer stock schemes

For volatile agricultural/commodity markets, a **buffer stock scheme** stabilises prices around a target range: the agency **buys and stores** when prices fall to the floor and **sells from stock** when prices rise to the ceiling.
- **Pros:** stabler farm incomes and consumer prices.
- **Cons:** high storage costs, risk of running out of stock or funds, hard to set the right price, perishability.

## Evaluation

Price controls trade one problem for another: maximum prices raise affordability but create shortages and black markets; minimum prices protect incomes but create surpluses and inefficiency. Effectiveness depends on **elasticities**, enforcement, and whether the control tackles the underlying cause. Governments often prefer subsidies/taxes or income support instead.

## Why it matters

Price controls are a direct, examinable application of demand & supply, surplus and elasticity — and a classic ‘government failure’ case where intervention creates new distortions.

keyTerms.definition: A legal upper limit on price, effective only if set below the equilibrium; causes shortages.
A legal lower limit on price, effective only if set above the equilibrium; causes surpluses.
When quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the controlled price.
When quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded at the controlled price.
An illegal market where goods are traded above a maximum price during a shortage.
A price-stabilisation scheme that buys surplus at a floor and sells stock at a ceiling.

diagrams: **Maximum price:** D/S diagram with a horizontal price line BELOW equilibrium; label the shortage as the horizontal distance between quantity demanded and quantity supplied.
**Minimum price:** D/S diagram with a horizontal price line ABOVE equilibrium; label the surplus (excess supply).
**Buffer stock:** show a target price band; buying adds to demand when price hits the floor, selling adds to supply when price hits the ceiling.
tips: State the golden rule: a maximum price only bites **below** equilibrium; a minimum price only bites **above** it. Getting this wrong loses the whole answer.
Always identify and **label the shortage or surplus** on the diagram, then explain the consequences (black markets, waste).
Bring elasticity in: the **size** of the shortage/surplus depends on the elasticities of demand and supply.
Evaluate with **alternatives** (subsidies, income support) and note the risk of **government failure**.
Use real examples: **New York City rent controls, Venezuela's price controls, the EU's farm price supports, Scotland's minimum alcohol price**, and national minimum wages.

channels.name: tutor2u Economics
channels.why: Clear max/min price diagrams and evaluation.

channels.name: EconplusDal
channels.why: Strong on price controls and buffer stocks.
